projects.title: Your First Filtered Query
projects.summary: Write a query to retrieve specific data from a single table with filtering and sorting.
projects.scenario: You have access to a customer database. Your manager asks: 'How many customers signed up in March 2025, and can you list them alphabetically?' You need to write a query to answer this.

sections.title: Connect and Explore the Table
content: First, connect to the database and understand the table structure. Before writing any query, look at what columns are available.
Always start by understanding the data. Running a quick exploratory query saves time later.

sections.title: Write the Filtered Query
content: Now write the query with explicit column names (not SELECT *), a WHERE clause for the date range, and ORDER BY for alphabetical sorting.
Notice we select specific columns, not everything. This is a good habit even for simple queries.
sections.codeSnippet:
SELECT first_name, last_name, email, created_at
FROM customers
WHERE created_at >= '2025-03-01'
  AND created_at < '2025-04-01'
ORDER BY last_name, first_name;

sections.title: Answer the Count Question
content: Your manager also asked 'how many.' A separate COUNT query gives the answer clearly.
At Level 1, running a separate count query is fine. Later you will learn to combine these.
sections.codeSnippet:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS march_signups
FROM customers
WHERE created_at >= '2025-03-01'
  AND created_at < '2025-04-01';

projects.title: Debugging a Broken Query
projects.summary: Find and fix errors in a SQL query by reading error messages.
projects.scenario: A colleague wrote a query that doesn't work. They get an error but can't figure out what's wrong. You need to read the error, identify the problem, and fix it.

sections.title: Read the Error Message
content: The broken query and its error message are your starting point. Read the error carefully — SQL error messages usually tell you exactly what's wrong and where.
Don't just scan the query visually. Match the error message to the specific part of the query it references.
sections.codeSnippet:
-- The broken query:
SELECT first_name last_name, email
FROM customer
WHERE signup_date = '2025-03-15'
ORDER BY last_name

-- Error: relation "customer" does not exist
-- (table is actually named 'customers')

sections.title: Fix the Errors and Verify
content: This query has three errors: wrong table name ('customer' vs 'customers'), missing comma between first_name and last_name, and missing semicolon. Fix them all.
After fixing, always run the query and verify the results make sense.
sections.codeSnippet:
-- Fixed query:
SELECT first_name, last_name, email
FROM customers
WHERE signup_date = '2025-03-15'
ORDER BY last_name;

projects.title: Monthly Revenue Report
projects.summary: Write a multi-table analytical query with JOINs, aggregation, and a CTE.
projects.scenario: The finance team needs a monthly revenue report broken down by product category. You need to join orders, order items, and products, then aggregate by month and category.

sections.title: Plan the Join Path
content: Before writing, identify the tables and how they connect. Orders have order_items, order_items reference products, products belong to categories.
Draw the join path: orders -> order_items -> products -> categories. Each join is through a foreign key.
sections.codeSnippet:
-- Join path:
-- orders.id = order_items.order_id
-- order_items.product_id = products.id
-- products.category_id = categories.id

sections.title: Write the Query Using a CTE
content: Use a CTE to compute the line-item revenue first, then aggregate by month and category in the outer query.
Check row counts at each step. After the JOIN, verify no accidental row multiplication by comparing against expected order_items count.
sections.codeSnippet:
WITH line_revenue AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_date) AS month,
    c.name AS category,
    oi.quantity * oi.unit_price AS line_total
  FROM orders o
  JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.id
  JOIN products p ON p.id = oi.product_id
  JOIN categories c ON c.id = p.category_id
)
SELECT
  month,
  category,
  SUM(line_total) AS revenue,
  COUNT(*) AS line_items
FROM line_revenue
GROUP BY month, category
ORDER BY month DESC, revenue DESC;

projects.title: Top Customers by Region
projects.summary: Use window functions to rank customers within regions by total spending.
projects.scenario: Marketing wants to identify the top 3 customers in each region for a VIP program. You need to rank customers by spending within their region and return only the top 3.

sections.title: Compute Total Spending per Customer
content: First CTE: aggregate total spending per customer by joining customers and orders.
sections.codeSnippet:
WITH customer_spending AS (
  SELECT
    c.id AS customer_id,
    c.name,
    c.region,
    SUM(o.total_amount) AS total_spent
  FROM customers c
  JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
  GROUP BY c.id, c.name, c.region
)

sections.title: Rank Within Region and Filter Top 3
content: Use DENSE_RANK() to handle ties (two customers with the same spend get the same rank). Filter to rank <= 3 in the outer query.
Decision: DENSE_RANK vs ROW_NUMBER. DENSE_RANK is correct here because ties should share a rank. ROW_NUMBER would arbitrarily break ties.
sections.codeSnippet:
WITH customer_spending AS (
  SELECT c.id, c.name, c.region,
         SUM(o.total_amount) AS total_spent
  FROM customers c
  JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
  GROUP BY c.id, c.name, c.region
),
ranked AS (
  SELECT *,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (
      PARTITION BY region ORDER BY total_spent DESC
    ) AS rank
  FROM customer_spending
)
SELECT name, region, total_spent, rank
FROM ranked
WHERE rank <= 3
ORDER BY region, rank;

projects.title: Design a Room Booking Schema
projects.summary: Design a normalized schema for a room booking system with constraints and migrations.
projects.scenario: A coworking space needs a booking system. Users book rooms for time slots. Bookings can be cancelled. Rooms have different capacities and amenities. Design the schema.

sections.title: Identify Entities and Relationships
content: Start with the entities: Users, Rooms, Bookings, Amenities. Rooms have amenities (many-to-many). Bookings connect Users to Rooms for a time slot.
Normalize to 3NF: room amenities go in a junction table, not a comma-separated list.
sections.codeSnippet:
-- Entity relationships:
-- users (1) --< (many) bookings
-- rooms (1) --< (many) bookings
-- rooms (many) >--< (many) amenities (via room_amenities)

sections.title: Write the DDL with Constraints
content: Every table needs appropriate constraints: NOT NULL for required fields, FOREIGN KEY for relationships, CHECK for business rules (end_time > start_time), UNIQUE where needed.
The booking table uses an exclusion constraint to prevent overlapping bookings for the same room.

sections.title: Write Reversible Migration Scripts
content: Wrap the DDL in up/down migration scripts. The down script should drop tables in reverse dependency order.

projects.title: Execution Plan Analysis
projects.summary: Read an execution plan, identify a missing index, and verify the improvement.
projects.scenario: A query that lists recent bookings for a specific user is slow. You need to analyze the execution plan, identify the problem, and propose a fix.

sections.title: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE
content: Run the slow query with EXPLAIN ANALYZE to see the actual execution plan and timings.
sections.codeSnippet:
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT b.id, r.name AS room, b.start_time, b.end_time
FROM bookings b
JOIN rooms r ON r.id = b.room_id
WHERE b.user_id = 42
  AND b.start_time >= NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'
ORDER BY b.start_time DESC;

sections.title: Identify the Problem and Add an Index
content: The plan shows a Seq Scan on bookings filtered by user_id and start_time. With thousands of bookings, this scans every row.
A composite index on (user_id, start_time) lets the database find the user's bookings and filter by time efficiently.
sections.codeSnippet:
-- Create the composite index
CREATE INDEX idx_bookings_user_time
  ON bookings (user_id, start_time DESC);

-- Re-run EXPLAIN ANALYZE to verify
-- Should now show Index Scan using idx_bookings_user_time

projects.title: Designing an Index Strategy
projects.summary: Design a complete index strategy for a workload of 5 common queries.
projects.scenario: An e-commerce platform has 5 frequently-run queries against its orders schema. You need to design an index strategy that covers all of them without over-indexing.

sections.title: Analyze the Query Workload
content: List the 5 queries and identify which columns they filter, join, and sort on. Look for overlapping patterns.
A good index strategy shares indexes across queries where possible.
sections.codeSnippet:
-- Query 1: Recent orders by customer
--   WHERE customer_id = ? AND created_at > ?
--   ORDER BY created_at DESC

-- Query 2: Order status lookup
--   WHERE customer_id = ? AND status = ?

-- Query 3: Daily revenue report
--   WHERE created_at BETWEEN ? AND ?
--   GROUP BY DATE(created_at)

-- Query 4: Fulfillment queue
--   WHERE status = 'pending' ORDER BY created_at

-- Query 5: Customer order history
--   WHERE customer_id = ? ORDER BY created_at DESC

sections.title: Design the Index Set
content: Queries 1, 2, and 5 all filter on customer_id. A composite index (customer_id, created_at DESC) covers queries 1 and 5. Query 2 needs (customer_id, status).
Query 3 filters on created_at alone. Query 4 filters on status + created_at. Design each index with column order matching the query pattern.
Document trade-offs: each index adds write overhead. Verify no redundancy.
sections.codeSnippet:
-- Index strategy:
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_customer_date
  ON orders (customer_id, created_at DESC);
  -- Covers: Query 1, Query 5

CREATE INDEX idx_orders_customer_status
  ON orders (customer_id, status);
  -- Covers: Query 2

CREATE INDEX idx_orders_created
  ON orders (created_at);
  -- Covers: Query 3

CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status_date
  ON orders (status, created_at)
  WHERE status = 'pending';
  -- Covers: Query 4 (partial index)

-- Trade-off: 4 indexes on one table.
-- Write overhead is acceptable because orders are
-- written once but queried many times.

projects.title: Isolation Level Experiment
projects.summary: Demonstrate concurrency anomalies and how isolation levels prevent them.
projects.scenario: You need to teach your team about isolation levels. Set up a hands-on demonstration showing a non-repeatable read and how REPEATABLE READ prevents it.

sections.title: Set Up the Demonstration
content: Open two database sessions (Session A and Session B). Create a simple test table.

sections.title: Demonstrate the Anomaly
content: In READ COMMITTED (default), Session A reads the balance, Session B updates and commits, Session A reads again and sees a different value.
Then repeat with REPEATABLE READ to show the anomaly is prevented.

projects.title: Multi-Service Data Architecture
projects.summary: Design data architecture for a system migrating from a monolithic database to microservices.
projects.scenario: Your company is splitting a monolithic application into 4 services: Users, Orders, Inventory, and Payments. All currently share one PostgreSQL database. Design the target data architecture.

sections.title: Define Data Ownership Boundaries
content: Each service owns its data. No service directly queries another service's tables. Identify which tables belong to which service.
Shared reference data (e.g., country codes) needs a clear ownership strategy.
sections.codeSnippet:
-- Data ownership:
-- Users Service:   users, user_addresses, user_preferences
-- Orders Service:  orders, order_items, order_status_history
-- Inventory:       products, stock_levels, warehouses
-- Payments:        payments, refunds, payment_methods

-- Shared reference: countries, currencies
-- Decision: owned by Users service, replicated read-only to others

sections.title: Design the Consistency Strategy
content: Cross-service operations (e.g., placing an order checks inventory and creates a payment) need a consistency approach.
Decision: use the Saga pattern with compensating transactions rather than distributed 2PC. Document the trade-offs.
sections.codeSnippet:
-- Order placement saga:
-- 1. Orders service: create order (status = 'pending')
-- 2. Inventory service: reserve stock
--    - If fails: Orders cancels order (compensating tx)
-- 3. Payments service: process payment
--    - If fails: Inventory releases stock, Orders cancels
-- 4. Orders service: confirm order (status = 'confirmed')

-- Trade-off: eventual consistency between services.
-- An order may be 'pending' for seconds during the saga.
-- Acceptable for this use case; payment-critical paths
-- have explicit compensation logic.

sections.title: Plan the Migration Path
content: Migration from monolith to microservices happens in phases. Start by extracting the service with the fewest cross-dependencies.
Use the Strangler Fig pattern: new services read from the monolith initially, then gradually take over writes.
sections.codeSnippet:
-- Migration phases:
-- Phase 1: Extract Users service
--   - New users DB with same schema
--   - Dual-write during transition
--   - Verify data consistency
--   - Cut over reads, then writes
--
-- Phase 2: Extract Inventory
--   - Products move to inventory DB
--   - Orders service uses API instead of JOIN
--
-- Phase 3: Extract Payments
-- Phase 4: Orders becomes standalone
--
-- Each phase: 2-4 weeks with rollback capability

projects.title: Establishing Data Governance
projects.summary: Create a data governance framework including naming conventions, review process, and quality metrics.
projects.scenario: Your 30-person engineering team has inconsistent database practices: some use snake_case, others camelCase; migrations aren't always reviewed; there's no documentation standard. You need to establish governance.

sections.title: Define Naming Conventions
content: Establish clear, enforceable naming rules for all database objects. Document with examples.
The convention should be opinionated but practical — the goal is consistency, not perfection.
sections.codeSnippet:
-- Naming Convention Standard
--
-- Tables: snake_case, plural (users, order_items)
-- Columns: snake_case, singular (first_name, created_at)
-- Primary keys: id (serial or UUID)
-- Foreign keys: {referenced_table_singular}_id (user_id)
-- Indexes: idx_{table}_{columns} (idx_orders_customer_date)
-- Constraints: chk_{table}_{rule} (chk_bookings_end_after_start)
-- Booleans: is_{adjective} or has_{noun} (is_active, has_verified_email)
-- Timestamps: {verb}_at (created_at, updated_at, deleted_at)

sections.title: Design the Review Process
content: Every schema change needs review. Define who reviews, what they check, and what blocks a merge.
Automate what you can: linters catch naming violations, CI verifies migrations run forward and backward.
sections.codeSnippet:
-- Schema Change Review Checklist:
-- [ ] Migration runs forward and backward without errors
-- [ ] Naming follows convention (enforced by linter)
-- [ ] All new columns have NOT NULL or explicit DEFAULT
-- [ ] Foreign keys include ON DELETE behavior
-- [ ] New indexes justified by query workload
-- [ ] Backward-compatible (old code still works)
-- [ ] Data backfill plan documented if needed
-- [ ] Estimated lock time for ALTER on large tables
--
-- Approval: 1 reviewer for additive changes
--           2 reviewers for destructive changes (DROP, ALTER type)
